title: Understand: Amending the Constitution
description: Check your understanding of the process to amend the Constitution as written in Article V.
topic_path: Civil Dialogue, The Constitution, and Constitutional Interpretation

assessment_items.question_content:
Article V outlines a formal process that has allowed later generations to revise the Constitution without resorting to violence or revolution. 

**What is this process called?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The impeachment process
The amendment process
The midterm election process
The judicial review process

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which article of the Constitution outlines the process for adding amendments like the Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Article I
Article II
Article III
Article V

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the two steps required for passing an amendment to the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Proposal by Congress; signature of the president
Proposal by state legislatures or state conventions; approval by the Supreme Court
Proposal by Congress or the states; ratification by state legislatures or state conventions
Proposal by the president; popular vote in all 50 states

assessment_items.question_content:
**The process for adding new amendments is outlined by Article V of the Constitution. The Founding generation believed that this process should be \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: very quick and easy, in order to ensure that the law could respond to social change
possible in theory, but impossible in practice
easy for the wealthy, but difficult for ordinary citizens
difficult, in order to encourage thoughtful deliberation and require broad support

assessment_items.question_content:
Both houses of Congress have voted by a two-thirds supermajority to propose a new amendment. 

**Which methods might Congress use to ratify the amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Three-fourths of state legislatures vote to ratify
Three-fourths of special state ratifying conventions vote to ratify
Three-fourths of both houses of Congress vote to ratify
Three-fourths of all eligible voters vote to pass

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the TWO methods for proposing a constitutional amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Two-thirds of states call for a national convention for proposing an amendment
Two-thirds of Congress approves of proposing an amendment
The president signs an executive order proposing an amendment
A single state calls a special state convention for proposing an amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best defines an amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A change to the Constitution
A new law passed by Congress
An order from the president
A legal document sent to the Court

assessment_items.question_content:
Since 1789, more than 11,000 amendments to the U.S. Constitution have been proposed, but only 27 have been ratified. 

**Which statement best explains this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The amendment process has a high bar for ratification.
Presidents are reluctant to sign off on amendments that pass Congress before they are sent to the states for ratification.
Most amendments fail to secure supermajority support from the American people in a national ratification vote.
The Supreme Court needs to approve any amendments for ratification.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What CANNOT be changed by the ordinary amendment process laid out in Article V?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The number of representatives a state has in the Senate
The total number of electors in the Electoral College
The term limits of members of Congress
The powers of the three branches of the federal government

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which part of the federal government is responsible for choosing the mode of ratification for an amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The judicial branch
The executive branch
The legislative branch
The federal bureaucracy

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which part of the government makes the ultimate decision to ratify a new amendment to the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Senate
The president
The Supreme Court
The states

assessment_items.question_content:
**How does the "convention method" for proposing amendments differ from the "congressional proposal method"?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The convention method requires presidential approval, while the congressional method does not.
The convention method requires a simple majority vote, while the congressional method requires a two-thirds vote.
The convention method is used for ratifying amendments, while the congressional method is used for proposing them.
The convention method is initiated by state legislatures, while the congressional method is initiated by Congress.
